other_side: NLW's lead is that Fable 5 is the best model we've ever used and a genuine leap that should make us dream bigger and develop "task imagination." Writing two days earlier, Zitron argues almost the reverse: the models keep shoving out a great deal of "stuff" that is unused, insecure slopware, the benchmarks are rigged, and the only thing reliably accelerating is cost. He reads the very token-efficiency turn NLW celebrates — Uber's 1,500-dollar-a-month cap, firms throttling spend — as evidence that "AI is slowing down," because customers recoil the moment they see the true bill. Where NLW sees ambition we are too timid to use, Zitron sees an unprofitable product whose revenue growth is stalling precisely when it must speed up.
truth_beneath: Strip the rhetoric and both men orbit one variable: whether rising token consumption reflects durable value or merely subsidised activity. They accept the same facts — Fable can one-shot work that once took teams, enterprises like Uber are capping token spend, and billing moved to usage in early 2026. The empirical question that will resolve is whether heavier agent use yields output enterprises keep paying for at unsubsidised prices; the observable that settles it is whether capped firms raise or cut their token budgets next, and whether shipped features and revenue can be traced to the agents. The residue is pure worldview: is "task imagination" a real skill gap or a marketing frame to drive token burn? NLW is vindicated if caps lift and real output follows; Zitron if caps harden as true costs bite. Both are polemicists — NLW bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype — and the signal is that both now fixate on the same Uber cap. Watch next quarter's enterprise token budgets.
